posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos
posts.cooked:
AN EVENING WITH KEVIN SHIN - Virginia (7/10/2022)

Great to see friend and talented wine taster Kevin Shin. Many thanks for the welcome, excellent conversation and the time to drink three wines, the second of which - a fine Premier Cru Chablis - I couldn’t find in Cellar Tracker. After having been opened for two days already, the Alain Gautheron Chablis Premier Cru “Les Fourneaux” 2019 was still lovely, with the as expected freshness and vibrancy, albeit a touch acacia and even honey, as the appellation would suggest. Indeed, the vineyard is named for its warm climate. Fourneaux means “furnace” in French. With a full on south exposure, it tends to yield very ripe grapes, as evidenced here. It is always reassuring that the wine can be opened for two days and still yield pleasure, not just be “acceptable” and as much as I loved that Champagne it was nice to taste this from a producer I had not heard of before. 92/100

  • 2006 Taittinger Champagne Comtes de Champagne Blanc de Blancs Brut* - France, Champagne
    This is drinking wonderfully. Many thanks to Kevin Shin. His peerless stocking conditions, the wine tasted almost as upon release, this vintage being broader, more evidently creamy than, say, the 2008 vintage. But as other professional tasters have noted, this is an exemplary effort for the warmer vintage that can sometimes exude tropical notes. Not so, here. What pleased me most was the impressive weight and palate feel, finely textured, with bright notes of citrus, subtle in expression with some brioche, albeit not really tertiary quite yet. I was focusing a bit more on our conversation so did not take careful notes, but we both loved it! Kevin served some excellent cheeses with crackers - and an addictive duck mousse, all of which paired very well with the Champagne, which showed a very fine bead, still rather youthful, again due to the provenance. (96 pts.)
  • 1992 Philip Togni Cabernet Sauvignon - USA, California, Napa Valley, Spring Mountain District
    After nearly 20 years (in bottle), truly fun to drink, with eucalyptus, mint and herbal aspects. About as far and away from a ripe blockbuster Cab as you can get, and although lacking the complexity of a superior wine, it was vibrant on the palate, with freshness, delivered in smooth tannin. I recall the alcohol being 12.3% (94 pts.)

As we approached the end of a fine - and not too hot - northern Virginia summer evening, we spoke about wine criticism in general, and how I think Kevin would make an excellent critic if he wanted to (he posts copious notes on Cellar Tracker), as his palate is more than a match for many professionals.

In any case, we spoke about how we met, via the old bulletin board on Robert Parker, pre-dating Neal Martin and Lisa Perrotti-Brown (and now William Kelley). In talking about that, Kevin said that the Philip Togni 1992 Cabernet Sauvgnon, the third and final wine we enjoyed, is a favorite of Kelley’s.

Many thanks, Kevin.

*2006 in Champagne is not considered a top vintage but some estates - like Taittinger’s Comtes de Champagne - did better than others. Acidity is not as high as in 2008, given hot June and July with heat wave days. Hail proved a problem but not sure the extent to which Taittinger’s special cuvée was affected in terms of volume. August brought humidity (also rot, mildew and some botrytis). However, Indian summer warm dry days helped phenolic ripeness with cooler evenings to preserve acidity.
Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: Sounds like a great evening! I love that 1992 Togni, probably the best Togni I have had, which is saying something. Have never found it lacking in complexity, those herbal elements can be intriguing!

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
Hi Panos,

in my opinion young whites often benefit from being open 1 or 2 days in the fridge.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
Hi Panos,
That was a lot of fun. I am in agreement with you. I liked the Togni a bit more than you, a classic old school textbook Togni.
Posts like this are some of the best things about this forum. Giving people with a shared passion a chance to connect with someone we might not otherwise have met.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
Hi Panos–so pleased to see you post here and that you got over the pond to share some time with Kevin.

We are hoping to get back to France (Burgundy and the Loire this time) next spring–maybe in May.

Hope you have otherwise been keeping well, safe and healthy.

Mike

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Kevin said that the Philip Togni 1992 Cabernet Sauvgnon, the third and final wine we enjoyed, is a favorite of Kelley’s.

Kevin is right! It’s probably my all-time favorite vintage of this bottling, and I was a little surprised to see you found it wanting in complexity. Togni availed themselves of the “Table Wine” tag in labelling so I don’t think the ABV is listed on the label, but I would have thought it’s more in the region of 13-13.5% than 12.3%. I think I have one bottle left. It’s probably the only bottle in Beaune.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Kevin said that the Philip Togni 1992 Cabernet Sauvgnon, the third and final wine we enjoyed, is a favorite of Kelley’s.

Kevin is right! It’s probably my all-time favorite vintage of this bottling, and I was a little surprised to see you found it wanting in complexity. Togni availed themselves of the “Table Wine” tag in labelling so I don’t think the ABV is listed on the label, but I would have thought it’s more in the region of 13-13.5% than 12.3%. I think I have one bottle left. It’s probably the only bottle in Beaune.

I had the 1992 Togni 2 weeks ago on a trip to Spain. Even after traveling with me, it showed brilliantly. I do not understand the comment about lacking the complexity of “a superior wine.” I agree with William that the '92 is possibly the best vintage made, and the estate is not one I’d ever label inferior. Nor has this wine ever lacked complexity, in my experience. By comparison, we also had 1992 Harlan and 1992 Diamond Creek Red Rock Terrace on this trip, and the Togni more than held its own. A shame your bottle did not please you as well.

posts.cooked: 92 Togni is also one of my all time favorites. Drank my last bottle a year or two ago and it was stellar with plenty of life left.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
I am going to jump on this '92 Togni bandwagon. When Sarah posted about her bottle a couple of weeks ago, I told her that the only Togni I have had was a bottle of '92 that someone brought to a party at my house and it was fantastic. Probably tied with '92 Montebello for the best '92 I have had.

I agree with Panos’ note on the Comtes and will add that it has drunk like that since release. I always thought of it as having the fruit of the '02 and the acidity of '04.

posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos
posts.cooked:
Kevin said that the Philip Togni 1992 Cabernet Sauvgnon, the third and final wine we enjoyed, is a favorite of Kelley’s.

Kevin is right! It’s probably my all-time favorite vintage of this bottling, and I was a little surprised to see you found it wanting in complexity. Togni availed themselves of the “Table Wine” tag in labelling so I don’t think the ABV is listed on the label, but I would have thought it’s more in the region of 13-13.5% than 12.3%. I think I have one bottle left. It’s probably the only bottle in Beaune.

I had the 1992 Togni 2 weeks ago on a trip to Spain. Even after traveling with me, it showed brilliantly. I do not understand the comment about lacking the complexity of “a superior wine.” I agree with William that the '92 is possibly the best vintage made, and the estate is not one I’d ever label inferior. Nor has this wine ever lacked complexity, in my experience. By comparison, we also had 1992 Harlan and 1992 Diamond Creek Red Rock Terrace on this trip, and the Togni more than held its own. A shame your bottle did not please you as well.

Thanks for the reply William (and Sarah). I guess this proves why scores can be tricky. We were at the time also discussing great Bordeaux from top estates, and my remark about complexity reflected such a (theoretical) comparison, but I probably underrated the score. Why? Because it was so darn enjoyable to drink, so let me update it to 95 rather than 94. And William is right about the alcohol level. It was getting dark on the patio, and I think I may have confused the alcohol level.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
The 92 Togni is complex in a “Californian” way. Perhaps, a different way to describe is that the wine lacks the finesse of the top Bordeauxs. Panos seldom get to try Napa wines so his context is a bit different.

  • 1992 Philip Togni Cabernet Sauvignon - USA, California, Napa Valley, Spring Mountain District (7/10/2022)
    Expressive nose displaying concentrated black fruit with a hint of red, crème de cassis, blackberry concentrate, plum, a hint of cherry, ash, smoke, noticeable green/pyrazine, lead pencil, leather, that I associate with an old school cal cab, eucalyptus and crushed rocks. Excellent concentration, layers upon layers of concentrated black fruit, unctuous and rich, bright acidity, strong mineral, and a long ripe black fruit driven finish with smoke and green at the end. It is both ripe and green which is really fascinating. (97 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
The complexity I have found in the 92 Togni in particular is layer on layer of herbal / vegetal / floral notes, lifted and supported by the deep fruit but a lot more complex than just fruit. Kevin may have been getting at this by saying it is both “ripe and green”. But that green-ness to me is a lot more complicated than just pepper or pyrazine, it’s got herbs and wildflowers etc. A California meadow .

I didn’t find it unctuous and it’s a world apart from massive overt size and sweetness you get in a lot of contemporary Napa cabs. Of course “unctuous” means different things to different people, I interpret it as somewhat cloying and sweet. But it definitely has that Napa sun-kissed quality in the background.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Kevin is right! It’s probably my all-time favorite vintage of this bottling, and I was a little surprised to see you found it wanting in complexity. Togni availed themselves of the “Table Wine” tag in labelling so I don’t think the ABV is listed on the label, but I would have thought it’s more in the region of 13-13.5% than 12.3%. I think I have one bottle left. It’s probably the only bottle in Beaune.

I had the 1992 Togni 2 weeks ago on a trip to Spain. Even after traveling with me, it showed brilliantly. I do not understand the comment about lacking the complexity of “a superior wine.” I agree with William that the '92 is possibly the best vintage made, and the estate is not one I’d ever label inferior. Nor has this wine ever lacked complexity, in my experience. By comparison, we also had 1992 Harlan and 1992 Diamond Creek Red Rock Terrace on this trip, and the Togni more than held its own. A shame your bottle did not please you as well.

Thanks for the reply William (and Sarah). I guess this proves why scores can be tricky. We were at the time also discussing great Bordeaux from top estates, and my remark about complexity reflected such a (theoretical) comparison, but I probably underrated the score. Why? Because it was so darn enjoyable to drink, so let me update it to 95 rather than 94. And William is right about the alcohol level. It was getting dark on the patio, and I think I may have confused the alcohol level.

Next time do it blind next to a 1990 Latour after a three hour decant for both wines! Did that in Oxford a decade ago and the Togni won. And not on the basis of richness/sweetness, as your tasting note very accurately observes.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The complexity I have found in the 92 Togni in particular is layer on layer of herbal / vegetal / floral notes, lifted and supported by the deep fruit but a lot more complex than just fruit. Kevin may have been getting at this by saying it is both “ripe and green”. But that green-ness to me is a lot more complicated than just pepper or pyrazine, it’s got herbs and wildflowers etc. A California meadow .

I didn’t find it unctuous and it’s a world apart from massive overt size and sweetness you get in a lot of contemporary Napa cabs. Of course “unctuous” means different things to different people, I interpret it as somewhat cloying and sweet. But it definitely has that Napa sun-kissed quality in the background.

For me, always a bit of mountain laurel, too. They are growing all around, after all.

Interestingly, during the great era of Mayacamas in the 1970s, apparently most of the fruit was sourced from a vineyard on Spring Mountain not that far from where Togni planted a little later. Makes you think…

Kevin said that the Philip Togni 1992 Cabernet Sauvgnon, the third and final wine we enjoyed, is a favorite of Kelley’s.

Kevin is right! It’s probably my all-time favorite vintage of this bottling, and I was a little surprised to see you found it wanting in complexity. Togni availed themselves of the “Table Wine” tag in labelling so I don’t think the ABV is listed on the label, but I would have thought it’s more in the region of 13-13.5% than 12.3%. I think I have one bottle left. It’s probably the only bottle in Beaune.

I had the 1992 Togni 2 weeks ago on a trip to Spain. Even after traveling with me, it showed brilliantly. I do not understand the comment about lacking the complexity of “a superior wine.” I agree with William that the '92 is possibly the best vintage made, and the estate is not one I’d ever label inferior. Nor has this wine ever lacked complexity, in my experience. By comparison, we also had 1992 Harlan and 1992 Diamond Creek Red Rock Terrace on this trip, and the Togni more than held its own. A shame your bottle did not please you as well.

The 1992 Harlan was made by Bob Egelhoff. He makes his own wine these days, they are some of my favorites, especially in the “off vintages”. Oh, they’re also about 1/10th the price of Harlan. He also made a few of the David Arthur Elevation 1147 vintages. He’s a hell of a nice guy that I was lucky to meet at the Kelham Christmas party in Napa I think around 2007/2008ish.

Great bottle.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Kevin said that the Philip Togni 1992 Cabernet Sauvgnon, the third and final wine we enjoyed, is a favorite of Kelley’s.

Kevin is right! It’s probably my all-time favorite vintage of this bottling, and I was a little surprised to see you found it wanting in complexity. Togni availed themselves of the “Table Wine” tag in labelling so I don’t think the ABV is listed on the label, but I would have thought it’s more in the region of 13-13.5% than 12.3%. I think I have one bottle left. It’s probably the only bottle in Beaune.

I was just getting into wine when this wine was hitting the shelves. I recall a boutique wine store had opened in my town and the owner was a huge fan of Togni. Started buying 91, 92, 93, etc. Dont recall the price but it was quite affordable on my young lawyer salary. I so distinctly recall the pronounced bell pepper notes on these wines. Shortly thereafter I’m getting into Chinon. My buddies and I didn’t know any better, we were drinking the stuff up like Kool-Aid, none of us ever thought to age these wines.

Nice notes, Panos and Kevin!

blind comparisons. Cheers !

posts.name: Br1an Th0rne
posts.cooked:
For my birthday party several years ago, I inserted the ‘92 Togni as a ringer in a lineup of mature Bordeaux – '82 Pichon Lalande, '82 Haut Brion, '82 Gruaud Larose, '90 Montrose, '90 Leoville Las Cases and '90 Lynch Bages – because I’ve always thought the ‘92 Togni bore a striking resemblence to the '82 Gruaud Larose. On this night they were nearly indistinguishable, and the '92 Togni more than held it’s own.

I’ve consumed more Togni than any other Napa wine, and I’ve usually found it to have more in common with the Northern Medoc than it’s California brethren, particularly vintages from the early 1990’s.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I’ve always thought the ‘92 Togni bore a striking resemblence to the '82 Gruaud Larose. On this night they were nearly indistinguishable, and the '92 Togni more than held it’s own.

I can see aspects of the comparison, but the important distinction is that the Togni is brett free!

posts.name: Lawrence Leichtman
posts.cooked:
AN EVENING WITH KEVIN SHIN - Virginia (7/10/2022)

Great to see friend and talented wine taster Kevin Shin. Many thanks for the welcome, excellent conversation and the time to drink three wines, the second of which - a fine Premier Cru Chablis - I couldn’t find in Cellar Tracker. After having been opened for two days already, the Alain Gautheron Chablis Premier Cru “Les Fourneaux” 2019 was still lovely, with the as expected freshness and vibrancy, albeit a touch acacia and even honey, as the appellation would suggest. Indeed, the vineyard is named for its warm climate. Fourneaux means “furnace” in French. With a full on south exposure, it tends to yield very ripe grapes, as evidenced here. It is always reassuring that the wine can be opened for two days and still yield pleasure, not just be “acceptable” and as much as I loved that Champagne it was nice to taste this from a producer I had not heard of before. 92/100

  • 2006 Taittinger Champagne Comtes de Champagne Blanc de Blancs Brut* - France, Champagne
    This is drinking wonderfully. Many thanks to Kevin Shin. His peerless stocking conditions, the wine tasted almost as upon release, this vintage being broader, more evidently creamy than, say, the 2008 vintage. But as other professional tasters have noted, this is an exemplary effort for the warmer vintage that can sometimes exude tropical notes. Not so, here. What pleased me most was the impressive weight and palate feel, finely textured, with bright notes of citrus, subtle in expression with some brioche, albeit not really tertiary quite yet. I was focusing a bit more on our conversation so did not take careful notes, but we both loved it! Kevin served some excellent cheeses with crackers - and an addictive duck mousse, all of which paired very well with the Champagne, which showed a very fine bead, still rather youthful, again due to the provenance. (96 pts.)
  • 1992 Philip Togni Cabernet Sauvignon - USA, California, Napa Valley, Spring Mountain District
    After nearly 20 years (in bottle), truly fun to drink, with eucalyptus, mint and herbal aspects. About as far and away from a ripe blockbuster Cab as you can get, and although lacking the complexity of a superior wine, it was vibrant on the palate, with freshness, delivered in smooth tannin. I recall the alcohol being 12.3% (94 pts.)

As we approached the end of a fine - and not too hot - northern Virginia summer evening, we spoke about wine criticism in general, and how I think Kevin would make an excellent critic if he wanted to (he posts copious notes on Cellar Tracker), as his palate is more than a match for many professionals.

In any case, we spoke about how we met, via the old bulletin board on Robert Parker, pre-dating Neal Martin and Lisa Perrotti-Brown (and now William Kelley). In talking about that, Kevin said that the Philip Togni 1992 Cabernet Sauvgnon, the third and final wine we enjoyed, is a favorite of Kelley’s.

Many thanks, Kevin.



*2006 in Champagne is not considered a top vintage but some estates - like Taittinger’s Comtes de Champagne - did better than others. Acidity is not as high as in 2008, given hot June and July with heat wave days. Hail proved a problem but not sure the extent to which Taittinger’s special cuvée was affected in terms of volume. August brought humidity (also rot, mildew and some botrytis). However, Indian summer warm dry days helped phenolic ripeness with cooler evenings to preserve acidity.
Posted from CellarTracker

Have not had the '92 Phillip Togni in several years but remember it fondly. Bought a half case in 95 but now all gone. Wish I had more.
